sources.title: NOAA Fisheries — Satellite Monitoring of Cook Inlet Beluga Whales (GAIA)
sources.used_for: GAIA program, acronym (Geospatial Artificial Intelligence For Animals), computer vision on very-high-resolution satellite imagery, 5 Cook Inlet monitoring areas, partners (NOAA, USGS, Microsoft AI for Good, Naval Research Laboratory), page updated 06/25/2026

sources.title: Marine Mammal Science — Adaptive Acoustic Monitoring for Endangered Cook Inlet Beluga Whales in Complex Soundscapes (Castellote et al.)
sources.used_for: the deep-learning passive-acoustic detector (robot ear), open-source dual-stage model, NOAA + Microsoft AI for Good authorship, online June 7 2026 / print July 2026
sources.credibility: peer-reviewed (verified via Crossref; full text paywalled)

sources.title: NOAA Fisheries — Beluga Whale (Cook Inlet DPS stock facts)
sources.used_for: population 331 (most-recent estimate), ~1,300 in 1979, 279 in 2018, nearly 80% decline 1979 to 2018, endangered under the ESA, decline drivers (ocean noise, prey, habitat, contaminants, predation, strandings)

sources.title: NOAA Fisheries — New Abundance Estimate for Endangered Cook Inlet Beluga Whales
sources.used_for: the 331 median estimate (2022 survey, range 290 to 386) corroboration

illustrative_note: On-screen population figures are NOAA's most-recent published estimates, not a live feed. The 331 count is NOAA's abundance estimate, not a real-time reading from either AI system.
